theme: "Civil Rights" OR "Civil Rights Movement"
title: Civil Rights Movement
description: Encyclopedia article about the civil rights movement in the American South, one of the most significant and successful social movements in the modern world. Black Georgians formed part of this southern movement for full civil rights and the wider national struggle for racial equality. From Atlanta to the most rural counties in Georgia's southwest Cotton Belt, black activists protested white supremacy in a myriad of ways--from legal challenges and mass demonstrations to strikes and self-defense. In many ways, the results were remarkable. As late as World War II (1941-45) black Georgians were effectively denied the vote, segregated in most areas of daily life, and subject to persistent discrimination and often violence. But by 1965, sweeping federal civil rights legislation prohibited segregation and discrimination, and this new phase of race relations was first officially welcomed into Georgia by Governor Jimmy Carter in 1971. The Civil Rights Digital Library received support from a National Leadership Grant for Libraries awarded to the University of Georgia by the Institute of Museum and Library Services for the aggregation and enhancement of partner metadata. GSE identifier: SS2H1

theme: "Civil Rights" OR "Civil Rights Movement"
title: Albany Civil Rights Movement Museum
description: The Albany Civil Rights Movement Museum opened in the Old Mt. Zion Baptist Church in Albany in 1998. The first mass meeting of the Albany Movement was held at the church in 1961, and in the 1990s the church building was converted into the museum. Known today as the Albany Civil Rights Institute, the museum moved to a new facility next to the church in 2008. Photograph of the Freedom Singers in front of the Old Mt. Zion Baptist Church, the location of the Albany Civil Rights Movement Museum in Albany, Georgia. It is a brick church with two low spires rising from the front. The former church was renovated in the 1990s, and the museum opened in 1998. The first mass meeting of the Albany Movement was held at the church in 1961. The Albany Movement was the first mass movement in the modern civil rights era to have as its goal the desegregation of an entire community, and it resulted in the jailing of more than 1,000 African Americans in Albany and surrounding rural counties.

theme: "Civil Rights" OR "Civil Rights Movement"
title: Modern Civil Rights Movement in Alabama
description: Encyclopedia article about the modern civil rights movement in Alabama which began with a single act of civil disobedience by Rosa Parks in Montgomery in 1955. It began to fade from the public eye a decade later, following the formation of the original Black Panther Party in Lowndes County. During the intervening years, Alabama was the site of some of the most defining events of the civil rights era. These events transformed the state and profoundly changed America. The Civil Rights Digital Library received support from a National Leadership Grant for Libraries awarded to the University of Georgia by the Institute of Museum and Library Services for the aggregation and enhancement of partner metadata.

theme: "Civil Rights" OR "Civil Rights Movement"
title: Catholicism and the Civil Rights Movement
description: Encyclopedia article about the Catholic Church's involvement with the civil rights movement in Alabama. Most of Alabama's white Catholics shared white southerners' racism and initially opposed the goals of the movement. They preferred order and stability instead of activism for integration and racial justice. Catholic teaching clearly opposed racial discrimination, however, and after the mid-1960s there was little sympathy for segregation. For most of the civil rights movement, the Catholic Church in Alabama remained on the margins of the debates over integration and focused on internal Church affairs. It took the 1965 Selma to Montgomery march for voting rights to draw the Church from the margins into the mainstream of the movement. The Civil Rights Digital Library received support from a National Leadership Grant for Libraries awarded to the University of Georgia by the Institute of Museum and Library Services for the aggregation and enhancement of partner metadata.

theme: "Civil Rights" OR "Civil Rights Movement"
title: Martin Luther King Jr. during Civil Rights Movement
description: King's interest in nonviolence became a central tenet of his leadership of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference and helped lead a young generation of African Americans to promote desegregation through peaceful sit-ins. Photograph of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. walking down a street with two unidentified men. He wears a long dark coat and tie and looks left. King, a Baptist minister and president of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC), was the most prominent African American leader in the civil rights movement of the 1950s and 1960s.

theme: "Civil Rights" OR "Civil Rights Movement"
title: Chicano Civil Rights Movement March in Downtown Denver, 1974
description: Digital copy of a film negative taken by photographer Juan Espinosa. The image depicts a march in Denver that took place on March 17, 1974 to commemorate the one-year anniversary of the police killing of Luis Junior Martinez and their subsequent attack on the apartment building owned by the Crusade for Justice, a Chicano civil rights organization co-founded by Rudolfo "Corky" Gonzales. Marchers in this photo walk down 16th Street and hold posters and an Aztlan flag. Juan Espinosa was a journalism student at the University of Colorado in Boulder when he took this photograph. He won his first camera in a poker game at Da Nang Air Force Base in Vietnam while serving with the U.S. Air Force during the Vietnam War and, after his discharge in 1969, learned journalistic photography and darkroom techniques at Mesa State College in Grand Junction, Colorado. Espinosa enrolled at CU Boulder in 1971, joined the United Mexican Student Association (UMAS), and worked as a stringer for the Colorado Daily newspaper and as a photographer for UMAS Publications, which published Somos Aztlan magazine and newspaper by the same name. In the summer of 1972, Espinosa founded the El Diario student newspaper during the summer of 1972, which primarily covered the Chicano civil rights movement and UMAS activities on the CU Boulder campus. Between 1971-1974, Espinosa photographed the events and people of the Chicano civil rights movement in Colorado, Arizona, Texas, and California. In 1975, he and his...

theme: "Civil Rights" OR "Civil Rights Movement"
title: Primus King and the Civil Rights Movement historical marker
description: Location: 2nd Avenue just below 10th Street at Courthouse, Columbus Text of marker: "Primus King and the Civil Rights Movement. The modern Civil Rights Movement in Georgia began on July 4, 1944, when Primus E. King, an African-American barber and minister, attempted to vote at the Muscogee County Courthouse in the Democratic Party's primary election, which barred blacks from participating. King, a registered voter, was roughly turned away by a law enforcement officer. With the encouragement and financial backing of local activist Dr. Thomas Brewer, King filed suit in Federal court in Macon, arguing that excluding black voters was unconstitutional. The court ruled in King's favor, as did the U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals, ending Georgia's "whites only" primary. King's challenge eliminated the legal barriers to black voting in Georgia's state and local elections and set in motion a statewide black voter registration campaign that helped end disfranchisement and the system of Jim Crow discrimination. 2015.1 Erected by the Georgia Historical Society, the City of Columbus, Historic Columbus, and the Georgia Department of Economic Development 106-3"

theme: "Civil Rights" OR "Civil Rights Movement"
title: Women and Tacomas Civil Rights Movement: Mrs. Bil Moss
description: The Bil Moss oral history interviews form the core of a class project by Kristina L. Walker for the Community History Project under the supervision of Professor Michael K. Honey. The contents of Walker's project are contained in a looseleaf binder, which includes the transcripts of the interviews, Walkers research paper, and twelve undated color photographs of Moss and Walker, presumably taken in Mosss home on the day of the interview. Also included are a number of photocopied news articles covering Mosss political career and civic activities, a collection of photocopied portraits and family pictures from the Moss family, and other research materials. Two CD-Rs contain the original sound recording of the interview and digital copies of the photographs and transcript documents.

theme: "Civil Rights" OR "Civil Rights Movement"
title: Organization, Rationality and Spontaneity in the Civil Rights Movement
description: This paper re-examines the Civil Rights Movement in Tallahassee, Florida. In doing so, Lewis Killian concludes that, while organization and planning are key variables, social movement theory must take into account spontaneity and emergence, and the transformation of pre-existing structures.

theme: "Civil Rights" OR "Civil Rights Movement"
title: Carol (Coronado) Blakely video interview and transcript
description: Oral history of Carol (Coronado) Blakely, interviewed by Jose 'Cha-Cha' Jimenez, on 10/19/2012 about the Young Lords in Lincoln Park. In the video, an older white woman in a blue top speaks while looking towards the interviewer who is off camera. In the interview, Carol discusses growing up on the north side of Chicago, and her involvement as a young adult in the Young Patriots Organization, which organized a health clinic for underserved Chicago residents. She also discusses collaborating with members of the Young Lords and Black Panthers on community organizing. The transcript file includes a detailed biographical note about the subject.

theme: "Racial segregation" OR "Jim Crow" OR "Desegregation"
title: Segregation (Jim Crow)
description: Encyclopedia article about segregation, the legal and social system of separating citizens on the basis of race. The system maintained the repression of black citizens in Alabama and other southern states until it was dismantled during the civil rights movement in the 1950s and 1960s and by subsequent civil rights legislation. Segregation is usually understood as a legal system of control consisting of the denial of voting rights, the maintenance of separate schools, and other forms of separation between the races, but formal legal rules were only one part of the regime. Some historians list three other important elements contributing to the creation and reinforcement of the status quo: physical force and terror, economic intimidation, and psychological control exerted through messages of low worth and negativity transmitted socially to African American citizens. The Civil Rights Digital Library received support from a National Leadership Grant for Libraries awarded to the University of Georgia by the Institute of Museum and Library Services for the aggregation and enhancement of partner metadata.

theme: "Racial segregation" OR "Jim Crow" OR "Desegregation"
title: Radio Fights Jim Crow
description: Web site companion to a radio documentary of the same name produced by American Radio Works. The site is divided into several sections, each documenting a significant radio program and providing selections of the show. The documentary in its entirety is also provided in three parts. The first section documents a twenty-eight-week series of programs aired in 1938 and 1939 that "focused on a different ethnic group each week." In 1941 and 1942, a nine-part series explored black history and achievements in areas like music, science, industry, and sports." From 1944 to 1957, New World A'Coming was broadcast on a radio station in New York and showcased the work of African Americans before expanding to include other minority groups. Finally, Destination Freedom, which aired between 1948 and 1950 in Chicago, "showcased prominent characters and events in black history, while also examining contemporary political issues." The site also contains a slide show and a list of related resources. The Civil Rights Digital Library received support from a National Leadership Grant for Libraries awarded to the University of Georgia by the Institute of Museum and Library Services for the aggregation and enhancement of partner metadata.

theme: "Racial segregation" OR "Jim Crow" OR "Desegregation"
title: African Americans on Jim Crow in north
description: For more than half of the 20th Century racial segregation was legal across the American South. From the late 1800s up until to the 1960's blacks and whites were separated from one another in every important aspect of their lives. They attended different schools, rode in separate train cars, lived in different neighborhoods and couldn't marry each other. In the South there were brutal consequences for violating this color-line. These laws and rules went by a name: Jim Crow. Jim Crow laws didn't extend to the North, but blacks migrating here found a culture deeply embedded with racist attitudes. This week Minnesota Public Radio is presenting a documentary from American RadioWorks called "Radio Fights Jim Crow." As part of the project, MPR's Brandt Williams talked with a number of older African Americans in Minnesota about their memories of segregation. This report includes language which some listeners might find offensive.

title: Traveling in the Jim Crow South
description: Read the book, "Ruth and the Green Book." Introduce students to the "Negro Motorist Green Book," either through .pdf (attached) or through a copy of the book. Allow students to explore the lists of businesses and individuals who were willing to assist the African American travelers. Ask students to imagine that they are Ruth (or a brother), write a diary entry from the trip or a letter home to a friend telling them about the trip. Be Mr. Victor Green, write a page for your community--where could an African American traveler shop, rest, or eat in your community.

theme: "Racial segregation" OR "Jim Crow" OR "Desegregation"
title: Boston Confronts Jim Crow, 1890-1920
description: Boston, the headquarters of radical abolition during the antebellum period, is, paradoxically, often thought of as unfriendly to African-Americans today. In this study of the city's significant role in the fight against racism between 1890 and 1920, Mark Robert Schneider illuminates the vital links between Boston's antislavery tradition, race reform at the turn of the century, and the modern civil rights movement. Originally published by Northeastern University Press in 1997. With a new foreword by Zebulon Vance Miletsky.

theme: "Racial segregation" OR "Jim Crow" OR "Desegregation"
title: Reinstate the 7 Fighters Against Jim Crow
description: Round white pinback button purple printing, "REINSTATE THE 7 FIGHTERS AGAINST JIM CROW". In 1940 there was a student lead protest at N.Y.U. due to its football program holding star fullback, Leonard Bates, out of the coming contest against the University of Missouri. The students, known as the Bates Seven, were suspended from N.Y.U. for three months due to the protests. Bates was held out of the game due to a "Gentlemen's agreement" that black players would not play against teams that did not field black athletes due to discrimination. Currently not on view

title: [Floyd Arnold "Jim" Crow and Football Team]
description: Photograph of Floyd Arnold "Jim" Crow and a football team. The men in the first row, sitting cross legged, are, from left to right, unidentified, Ben Dean Sheats, Jack Southern, Garland Fawcett, and a man known as "Cheetah". The men in the second row are marked as Cook, Baughman, Curtis, La Grone, Marrs McLean, and Barney Hamm. Those in the last row are Jim Crow, a man known as "Deacon", Vasey, Goolachek, Mouser, Kazanas, and Lyday. Crow wears a jacket with the letter "B" on it, and Jack Southern holds a football in his lap. 1 photograph : b&w ; 21 x 26 cm.

theme: "Racial segregation" OR "Jim Crow" OR "Desegregation"
title: Civil rights, (Jim Crow Guide), 1949-1952
description: The Papers, 1933-1981, of Stetson Kennedy comprise correspondence; subject files on various organizations, individuals, and ideas; typescripts of articles written by Kennedy; newsclippings; press releases; bulletins and fliers; pamphlets; periodicals; and photographs. The subject files pertain to economic conditions, labor and anti-black violence, peace groups, peonage, Southern politicians, Mexico, the Spanish Civil War, and Kennedy's own campaign for a U.S. Senate seat from Florida in 1950. Articles, clippings, and pamphlets concern civil rights, international affairs, the Ku Klux Klan, labor (particularly CIO) organizing, and southern politics. The photographs depict WPA work in progress, attacks against Negroes (including lynching), and various organizations. The many periodicals include two issues (1947) of Eugene Talmadge's The Statesman, twenty-one issues (1943-1950) of The Southern Patriot, and eight issues (1939-1943) of Lillian Smith's North Georgia Review. The correspondence covers the period 1935-1979, and includes as correspondents students and peace groups, several committees to aid Spanish loyalists, social reform and civil liberties groups, government agencies, writer's organizations, publishers, literary agents, newspapers and magazines, and the New York Public Library, which obtained some Kennedy manuscripts for its Schomburg Collection in 1952. [L1979-37]

theme: "Racial segregation" OR "Jim Crow" OR "Desegregation"
title: Protester with sign, "Protest Jim Crow Police Power"
description: Established in 1946 as the educational arm of the Southern Conference for Human Welfare, the Southern Conference Educational Fund (SCEF) became a separate organization in 1947. Originally based in New Orleans, Louisiana, its office later moved to Louisville, Kentucky, then to Atlanta, Georgia. SCEF was a leading proponent of integration and civil rights in the South, but internal disputes and financial problems led to its demise in the 1980s. The records of the Southern Conference Educational Fund span 1958-1985 (bulk 1970-1981). They include correspondence, notes and minutes from SCEF Board and Interim Committee meetings, financial and fundraising information, reports from SCEF chapters, information on various SCEF programs and activities, court documents, and correspondence and indexes related the SCEF newspaper. Protester with sign, "Protest Jim Crow Police Power"

title: For the Rights of All: Ending Jim Crow in Alaska
description: The story of an extraordinary Alaskan woman who becomes an unlikely hero in the fight for civil rights. Elizabeth Peratrovich, an unassuming Tlingit Indian mother of three, testified before the Alaska Territorial Senate in 1945 and swayed the floor vote with her compelling testimony in favor of the passage of the Anti-Discrimination Act, the first civil rights bill passed in the United States since the Civil War.

theme: "Racial segregation" OR "Jim Crow" OR "Desegregation"
title: Bus desegregation in Atlanta
description: Encyclopedia article about the process and activity which led to bus desegregation in Atlanta, Georgia. In January 1957, following the successful bus boycott in Montgomery, Alabama (1955-56), a group of black ministers launched the "Love, Law, and Liberation" movement to desegregate Atlanta's city buses. Under the leadership of the Reverend William Holmes Borders, the ministers staged a violation of the state law requiring segregation on common carriers, thereby securing the grounds for a legal challenge to Georgia's Jim Crow system. Two years later, in January 1959, a federal district court ruled in favor of the ministers, ending more than six decades of segregation on Atlanta's city buses. The Civil Rights Digital Library received support from a National Leadership Grant for Libraries awarded to the University of Georgia by the Institute of Museum and Library Services for the aggregation and enhancement of partner metadata.

theme: "African American history" OR "African-American history" OR "Black history"
title: Documenting African American history
description: This clip features an interview with Byron Rushing, Director of the Museum of Afro-American History in Boston, on the aims of the museum and the difficulties in documenting African American history. Overall program 109 focuses on the seizure and transport of African men, women, and children to the United States and Atlantic coast colonies. The physical transport, called "Middle Passage," is illustrated via the use of a filmstrip on the topic prepared by the Afro Audiovisual Company of Boston. Additional segments include the "Historical Minute" with Georgia State Representative Julian Bond, "Information" on why the slave trade took place, a performance by mime Fred Johnson (Halim Adbur Rashid), a filmed segment with Byron Rushing, Director of the Museum of Afro-American History in Boston, on the aims of the museum and the difficulties in documenting African American history, segment interludes with Wolf (of the musical group Mandrill), Coffee Cave and Tasha Jones (then cast member of The Wiz); "Book Beat," and commentary by Sarah-Ann Shaw on Third World struggles for independence. Produced by Marita Rivero. Directed by Conrad White.

title: National Museum of African American History and Culture Opens
description: Chronology of Smithsonian History NMAAHC is the nineteenth Smithsonian Museum "A Century in the Making: The Journey to Build a National Museum," Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture (blog), Tumblr. August 24th, 2016, http://nmaahc.tumblr.com/post/149430396115/a-century-in-the-making-the-journey-to-build-a. "Culture Wars Won and Lost, Part II: The National African-American Museum Project," Radical History Review 70 (1998): 78-101. The Time Has Come: Report to the President and to the Congress". National Museum of African American History and Culture Plan for Action Presidential Commission, last modified April 2, 2003, http://nmaahceis.si.edu/documents/The_Time_Has_Come.pdf. Dodson, Howard. "A Place of our Own: The National Museum of African American History and Culture." Callaloo Vol. 38 No. 4. (2015): 729-741. The National Museum of African American History and Culture (NMAAHC) is opened on September 24, 2016 by President Barack Obama during a three day festival on the National Mall produced by Quincy Jones, a member of the museum's advisory board. The only national museum devoted exclusively to the documentation of African American life, history, and culture, its goals are 1) to provide an opportunity for those who are interested in African American culture to explore this history through interactive exhibitions; 2) to help all Americans see how their stories, their histories, and their cultures are shaped and informed by global influences;...

theme: "African American history" OR "African-American history" OR "Black history"
title: New Mexico in Focus; African American History in New Mexico
description: Episode Number: 716 This week on New Mexico in Focus, host Gene Grant speaks with three experts about African American history in New Mexico ahead of the new PBS documentary “The African Americans: Many Rivers to Cross,” by Henry Louis Gates Jr., which premiers on October 22. The six-hour series explores the evolution of the African-American people, as well as the multiplicity of cultural institutions, political strategies, and religious and social perspectives they developed — forging their own history, culture and society against unimaginable odds. Guests for the local segment include Rita Powdrell and Brenda Dabney, who co-founded the exhibit “New Mexico’s African American Legacy – Visible, Vital, and Valuable,” which has been traveling the state, as well as Diana Dorn-Jones, executive director of United South Broadway Corp. and founding member of the Anti-Racism Institute of the Southwest. The Line opinion panel discusses the latest developments in the state’s new rule governing copper mines. Attorney General Gary King, media mogul Ted Turner, and environmental groups are asking the state Court of Appeals to halt the new regulation approved by the Water Quality Control Commission, arguing it is unconstitutional. The Line also looks at the new Mission Graduate initiative launched this week with the goal of increasing the number of college and university graduates and certificate holders in the Albuquerque area by 60,000 by 2020. However, the state’s Lottery Scholarship...

theme: "African American history" OR "African-American history" OR "Black history"
title: Design for National Museum of African American History and Culture
description: Historic Images of the Smithsonian Image Courtesy of the National Museum of African American History and Culture's Website. An artist rendition of the design by architect David Adjaye of the Freelon Adjaye Bond/Smith Group for the new National Museum of African American History and Culture. The design features a museum that has glass walls and features a crown motif from a Yoruban sculpture. The National Museum of African American History and Culture (NMAAHC) is a Smithsonian Institution museum established in 2003. It will be built on the National Mall in Washington, D.C. and is set to open in 2015. National Museum of African American History and Culture Institutional History Division, Smithsonian Archives, 600 Maryland Avenue, SW, Washington, D.C. 20024-2520, SIHistory@si.edu

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Session 1B: El Movimiento and Activism at CU Boulder
description: "Chicana Leadership and Activism on CU's Campus and in the Colorado Chicano Movement." This presentation explores the leadership of several Chicana activists at CU Boulder before, during, and after the Los Seis de Boulder bombings, including Neva Romero, who died in the first bombing on May 27, 1974. This research is part of a larger effort led by Professor Montoya and her student research assistants to produce a work of scholarship documenting the political and community activism of Chicanas throughout Colorado. 00:00 Jessie De La Cruz begins the session; 03:45 Celeste Montoya begins the presentation titled "Chicana Leadership & Activism on CU's Campus and in the Colorado Chicano Movement"; 04:36 Celeste talks about intersectionality used to analyze Latina leaders and the challenges they encounter; 08:25 Celeste and her co-presenters provide the biographies of six Chicana activist students from CU Boulder. Celeste starts with Becky Marrujo; 12:35 Raquel Hernandez Guerrero talks about the background of Flor Lovato Saiz; 17:18 Maria Martinez talks about Priscilla Falcon; 21:07 Celeste talks about Neva Romera; 27:55 Celeste talks about Maria Isidorita; 33:10 Raquel talks about Deborah Espinosa; 39:00 Celeste concludes the presentation by highlighting the importance of each woman's story; 40:55 Jesse opens the presentation up for questions. Anthony asks if any of the activists discussed during the presentation were inspired by the 1951 Empire Zinc mining strike; 45:20 Jesse...

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Oral history of Geraldine Gonzales
description: Description of content: Geraldine Gonzales is a Chicana activist and widow of Rodolfo ìCorkyî Gonzales, co-founder of the Crusade for Justice. In this oral history, Geraldine recounts meeting Corky and how their married family life was shaped by their mutual involvement in the burgeoning Chicano Activism Movement- "El Movimiento" - of which Corky was considered one of various leaders in the Denver Area. Gonzales was born into a Spanish speaking of 12 children in Brighton, Colorado before moving to Denver to attend junior high. She decided to forgo going to high school in order to get a cleaning job with her sister and mother, but she eventually got her GED later in life. She discusses her first meeting with Corky at a teen dance in the late 40ís, and how her parents discouraged Geraldine from marrying Corky due to his outspoken, forward thinking observations on her familyís Chicano identity, which clashed with her parentís more conservative self identifications. After they were married, she recounts how Corky was inspired to join El Movimiento because of how their childrenís Chicano identity was devalued and erased within the public school system. She believes that Corkyís activism never put the family in any real danger, but the scariest moments were when the apartment building owned by the Crusade for Justice was burned and one of their members, Luis "Junior" Martinez, was shot. She concludes by describing how Corky was always there for his family despite his dedication to...

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Roberto Trevino discussing the Chicano Movement, Seattle, May 9, 1970
description: During the summer of 1970, Brown Berets from the University of Washington (UW) led a voter registration drive in the Yakima Valley, and helped push the United States Supreme Court to hear the case against Yakima Valley registrars who refused to permit Spanish-speaking Mexican-Americans to vote. The Brown Berets organized pickets and welfare protests, and interrupted community meetings to promote bills establishing a minimum wage for farm workers and to unionize migrant workers. One of the leaders of the Brown Berets, Angela Lilia Grajeda, said, "We thought we were Americans until the Anglos said, 'You are Mexicans.' But if you are not Anglo you are not up to the standard. White supremacy makes us a minority race." In this image Roberto Trevino, a UW senior from the Yakima Valley, discusses his activism as a Brown Beret and MEChA (Movimiento Estudiantil Chicano de Aztlán) member. Caption information source: "The Chicano Movement in Washington State, 1967-2006: Part 2: Chicano Cultural Awakening," by Oscar Rosales Castañeda, The Seattle Civil Rights & Labor History Project, University of Washington. Caption information source: "Viva La Causa! Our State's Silent Minority Is Quiet No More," by Hilda Bryant, Seattle Post-Intelligencer, May 24, 1970, pages 118-119.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Becky Hobbs at Conferencia - Chicanas en El Movimiento, Seattle, August 22, 1974
description: In this image of Rebecca "Becky" Hobbs, the University of Washington Minority Affairs Office staff assistant discusses the first annual Conferencia - Chicanas en El Movimiento (Women in the Movement conference), held over three days at El Centro de La Raza on Beacon Hill. The event was an initial step in establishing a state-wide network for job listings, education opportunities, legislative lobbying, and support. Ms. Hobbs was quoted as saying, "Employers don't seem to think there are any Chicanas in Seattle. We know others are out there and don't want to lose them. We want to keep Chicana talent here. But first we must provide some job-skill training, a log of information on day care, resume writing workshops - whatever is needed to get the Chicana out of the background." Another hope for the conference was to bridge the gap between Chicanas in Eastern and Western Washington. Amalia Garza, an attendee, said, "Since we are so different, we can tap that many more talents. If we solve the practical problems, we can deal with the philosophical." The event was hosted by the Chicano Division and MECHA at the University of Washington, and funded by the Chicano Mobile Institute through the University of Utah. Caption information source: "Chicanas: 'Now It's Our Turn,'" Seattle Post-Intelligence, August 23, 1974, page 44.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Joan Augerot at Conferencia - Chicanas en El Movimiento, Seattle, August 22, 1974
description: This image of Joan Augerot, a curriculum specialist, was taken at the first annual Conferencia - Chicanas en El Movimiento (Women in the Movement conference), held over three days at El Centro de La Raza on Beacon Hill. Ms. Augerot was a speaker at the conference. The event was an initial step in establishing a state-wide network for job listings, education opportunities, legislative lobbying, and support. Most of the women who organized the conference were employed, primarily in education. They said they often feel isolated from their ethnic "sisters." Another hope for the conference was to bridge the gap between Chicanas in Eastern and Western Washington. Amalia Garza, an attendee, said, "Since we are so different, we can tap that many more talents. If we solve the practical problems, we can deal with the philosophical." The conference was hosted by the Chicano Division and MECHA at the University of Washington, and funded by the Chicano Mobile Institute through the University of Utah. Caption information source: "Chicanas: 'Now It's Our Turn,'" Seattle Post-Intelligence, August 23, 1974, page 44.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Marta Cano at Conferencia - Chicanas en El Movimiento, Seattle, August 22, 1974
description: In this image of Marta Cano, the Seattle Public Schools administrative intern discusses the first annual Conferencia - Chicanas en El Movimiento (Women in the Movement conference) held over three days at El Centro de La Raza on Beacon Hill. "The Chicana is in a double bind. We're treated as a minority within our own culture. People take it for granted that we're out in the kitchen making tortillas. We're trying to find who we are and where we are. This is not a liberation conference as much as an information exchange. We're feminists, but in the context of our culture." Hosted by the Chicano Division and MECHA at the University of Washington, and funded by the Chicano Mobile Institute through the University of Utah, the event was seen as an initial step in establishing a state-wide network for job listings, education opportunities, legislative lobbying, and support. Another hope for the conference was to bridge the gap between Chicanas in Eastern and Western Washington. Amalia Garza, an attendee, said, "Since we are so different, we can tap that many more talents. If we solve the practical problems, we can deal with the philosophical." Caption information source: "Chicanas: 'Now It's Our Turn,'" Seattle Post-Intelligence, August 23, 1974, page 44.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Session 5B: The Chicano Movement at CU Boulder, 1971-1975: The Photography of Juan Espinosa
description: Join CU alum and journalist Juan Espinosa (BA Journalism ‘74) for an audio-visual presentation of his photojournalism work documenting El Movimiento at CU Boulder, in Colorado, and across the West in the early 1970s. As an undergraduate at CU, Espinosa was the founder of El Diario de la Gente, UMAS’s student newspaper, and also served as one of its photographers and reporters. He was also the first student co-director of CU’s UMAS-Equal Opportunity Program (UMAS-EOP). Espinosa’s photographs document, among many other topics, conflicts within the UMAS-EOP program, the March 1973 police attack on the Crusade for Justice headquarters in Denver, La Raza Unida’s National Convention, the Constitutional Convention of the United Farm Workers Union, the May 1974 student occupation of CU’s Temporary Building 1, and the aftermath of the deaths of Los Seis de Boulder. 08:16 Jessica Ordaz introduces the presentation and speaker; 11:00 Juan Espinosa begins his presentation. He starts by playing a video he created using photographs he took during the Chicano movement from 1971-1975; 15:00 Juan starts the video again with the sound; 34:10 The video addresses the support of UMAS for the Black Student Alliance (BSA); 38:40 The video presentation displays graphic images of the aftermath of the car bombs; 39:16 Video presentation shows portraits of the Los Seis victims starting with Neva Romero, Reyes Martinez, Una Jaakola, Florencio "Freddy" Granado, Heriberto Lee Teran, and Francisco...

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Session 3A: Digital El Diario: Digital Care-Work for the History of El Movimiento
description: Digital El Diario is a digital humanities project that aims to dynamically represent the text and images published in El Diario de la Gente, the UMAS student newspaper founded in 1972. The newspaper is an extraordinary window into El Movimiento in Boulder and beyond, and its digitization on the Colorado Historic Newspaper Collection database has enabled infinite possibilities for research and teaching, including computational approaches for interactivity. Join Professor Eichmann-Kalwara and graduate students from her class, “Introduction for Digital Humanities: Movements, Methods, and Tools,” for a discussion of minimal computing for maximal justice, digital care-work, and the project’s goals to both decolonize the historical record towards archival justice and serve as an experiential learning opportunity to create humanities data. 00:00 Ashlyn Velte introduces the session and presenters; 02:50 Nickoal Eichmann-Kalwara begins her group's presentation titled "Digital El Diario: Digital care-work with the history of El Movimiento." Nickoal talks about the El Diario de la Gente newspaper which served as the main influence for her group's research project; 10:00 Nickoal talks about coming across the newspaper in the archive initially and not fully understanding the historical significance of the paper. She continues to talk about digital care-work; 13:35 Nickoal talks about using minimal computing, a method used in digital humanities, to highlight maximal justice in collections...

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Chicano Movement in Washington: Political Activism in the Puget Sound and Yakima Valley Regions, 1960s-1980s
description: In the late 1960s, the Mexican-American civil rights movement flourished throughout the United States, in 1967 making its presence known in Washington's Yakima Valley. A dramatic shift occurred in the Chicana/o and Latina/o community in Eastern Washington as a previously silent population raised its voice to advocate labor rights and social equity. When Yakima-area students recruited by the University of Washington made their way to Seattle, they brought the energy to initiate the Chicano student movement. The dualistic geography was reflected in the movement's activities, uniting the farm workers' struggle in Eastern Washington with campaigns targeting community and educational objectives in Western Washington. Chicano youth, particularly students at the University of Washington and at campuses throughout the state, generated much of this activism. Students formed local chapters of the United Mexican American Students, the Brown Berets, and Movimiento Estudiantil Chicano de Aztlan (MEChA), among other organizations. They spearheaded the formation of various organizations, committees, programs, and activities, both on and off-campus such as the United Farm Workers Cooperative and the United Farm Workers grape boycott, El Centro de La Raza, community health centers, and the Chicano Education Opportunity Program (EOP) and Chicano Studies center at the University of Washington...[View full item to read remainder of encyclopedia article.]

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Carmen Arteaga Oral History Interview
description: Carmen Arteaga discusses experiences as a teacher within Pueblo School District-60 in the 1970s. Particularly, when she taught at Parkview Elementary. She talks about her mission to incorporate the history of Chicanos into the curriculum. She also discusses her involvement with student walk outs at schools like Centennial High School. The walk outs were created to request more Chicano teachers and counselors. She goes on to discuss a Class Action lawsuit levied against the school district by residents of the Salt Creek community. The Office of Civil Rights would go onto take the case.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Dr. Ron Maestas Oral History Interview
description: Dr. Ronald Maestas talks about growing up in the San Luis Valley and graduating from Adams State College (now Adams State University) where he earned his bachelor’s and master’s degrees and later his Ph.D. at Arizona State University in Tempe Arizona. He talks about working at Highlands University in Las Vegas New Mexico as a professor and as the Dean of Business for forty years. He applied at Adams State College and when he was interviewed, he was met by blatant discrimination and racism. He wrote, “105 Years of Discrimination,” because of incidents like this throughout his life. He remembers signs in the San Luis Valley, that read, “No dogs or Mexicans allowed.” He talks about the 1913 discrimination case against the Alamosa school where a young, boy, Miguel Antonio Maestas was told he had to go to the Mexican School instead of the American School. The Maestas family won the case and recently a statue was dedicated to the case. He talks about how Hispanics/Mexicans/Chicanos have served their country in many wars and have made the ultimate sacrifice, especially in Normandy. He also talks about how the unique Spanish language spoken in the San Luis Valley is being lost by new generations. Dr. Maestas talks about the advances Hispanics/Mexicans/Chicanos have made because of the Chicano Movement, such as the Crusade for Justice led by Corky Gonzales and the Boycott against Coors. He talks about Dolores Huerta with the farmworkers and her slogan, “Si se Puede,” meaning "yes you...

title: Dario Madrid Oral History Interview - Transcript
description: Dario Madria discusses the dissolution of Chicano communites. He also discusses how he was one of the founders of the La Gente Community Center. The interview includes a discussion about Dario's involvement with the La Gente Boxing Team. He also tells stories about how he provided security for activists who were receiving death threats.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Margarito Fuentes Oral History Interview
description: Margarito Fuentes talks about his growing up in Pueblo, Colorado. His counselor encouraged him to go to CSU-Fort Collins under Project Go in 1970, where he met his wife who had entered this program in 1969. The program was to encourage Chicanos to attend college. He and his wife moved to Pueblo and graduated from CSU-Pueblo, then known as the University of Southern Colorado. This is when he met Martin Serna who was a Brown Beret with the Chicano Movement. He joined the Pueblo School Blowouts at Centennial High School which were protests to get equal treatment for Chicanos in education. He talks about Chicano Movement organizations such UMAS and MECHA that existed to also help get more equity in education. He joined La Cucaracha newspaper as a photographer. He talks about his career on the Santa Fe Railroad, first working as a trackman and later as a brakeman. He talks about how hard it was for Chicanos to be hired in positions other than laborers building track on the railroad because of prejudice and racism. He gave up the railroad and became an educator and taught at Central High School as an English & Chicano Studies instructor and a media and library specialist. He was instrumental in modernizing the classrooms with technology.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Agustine (Tito) Vega Oral History Interview - Transcript
description: Agustine (Tito) Vega dicusses his life growing up in Pueblo, Colorado. It includes his life as a student at the University of Colorado, Boulder and the University of Southern Colorado. It also includes his involvement as a Brown Beret in Pueblo, CO.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Dr. Richard Rivera Oral History Interview
description: Dr. Richard Rivera discusses his growing up in poverty in northern Colorado where his family had moved from Trinidad to work the beet fields of northern Colorado. His father was in World War II, and he came back dysfunctional. They lived in poverty mostly in abandoned houses and even lived under a tree near a river at one time. His father rarely held a job. In spite of all this, Dr. Rivera joined the Navy after getting married at the age of 18. He saw it as a way out of poverty. He was stationed in Parris Island, SC and completed his GED. After his service to the Navy, he enrolled at So. Colorado State College in Pueblo, where he attended for three years before being accepted to Harvard University where he received his medical degree and also, a master’s degree in public health in 1974. After graduating from Harvard, he practiced in California and Arizona and was asked to return to Pueblo Colorado to take over the leadership of the Pueblo Neighborhood Centers in 1975. He talks about how the centers became a threat to the Pueblo medical establishments, and he came under unrelenting attacks from his foes and other parts of the Pueblo power structure including District Attorney, Joe Losavio, who had his centers raided. He also became the victim of "red baiting." While in charge of the health centers he served over 40,000 clients. His model was to be open 7 days a week and after hours from different from other clinics. Dr. Rivera eventually gave up running the health centers to...

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: David Marquez Oral History Interview
description: Dave Marquez, Chicano Studies high school and university instructor for more than thirty years talks about his growing up in Pueblo and the racism he witnessed in the schools against Chicanos. He talks about him wanting to go to college and his school counselor, in spite of very high ACT scores, was advising him to go to welding school and that it was an honorable profession. Through his perseverance and the government mandating colleges increase minority enrollment he graduated from Colorado College in Colorado Springs. Dave was directly involved with the school walkouts in Pueblo where Chicano students fought for better education, and they forced the school district to comply with several mandates. Dave talks about his respect for the Brown Berets and that it is them that are the unsung heroes of the Chicano Movement. With his Chicano Study students, they published a newspaper called La Azteca where through good journalism uncovered the miscount of the Chicano dropout rate by the school district. He talks fondly about his students and the classes he taught both at Central High School and CSU-Pueblo. Dave describes the leadership of one of his Chicano Studies students that brought about change in how the university disperses the funds they get as a Hispanic Serving Institute (HSI). Dave talks about how the Chicano Movement helped professionals advance and get elected to powerful positions in Pueblo. He ends with hope but hopes that the young people realize the struggles...

description: Jose Esteban is a Chicano activist, CU UMAS/EOP leader, and Pueblo, Colorado native.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Cynthia Fuentes Oral History Interview
description: Cynthia Fuentes talks about growing up in Trinidad Colorado and the start of her higher education at CSU-Fort Collins in the early 70s and moving with her husband to Pueblo, Colorado where they both graduated from CSU-Pueblo. She talks about her self-identity as a Chicana and her promotion of the Chicano Movement. She was part of several protests including the Grape Boycott. She talks about her life as an educator and with the help of Superintendent Edmund Vallejos in the Pueblo school district became one of the first elementary guidance counselors. She received her degree in elementary guidance counseling from Adams State University. The first school she was given an opportunity to work at as a counselor was Bradford Elementary under the mentorship of Lucia Martinez Rivera. She was part of a crafts program at Quinto Sol Recreation Center called "Hecho en Aztlan." She explains her definition of "Aztlan," and the richness of Mexican Folklorico dancing.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: UMAS—EOP student, activist, and one time President, Florencio "Freddy" Granado speaks to students with the Mexican flag proudly displayed. Holding the flag on its right is Danny Vallez (Teatro de Ustedes member and CU UMAS/EOP)
description: Alurista is known as the author of the famous “Plan Espiritual de Aztlán.”

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Margarito Fuentes Oral History Interview - Transcript
description: Margarito Fuentes talks about his growing up in Pueblo, Colorado. His counselor encouraged him to go to CSU-Fort Collins under Project Go in 1970, where he met his wife who had entered this program in 1969. The program was to encourage Chicanos to attend college. He and his wife moved to Pueblo and graduated from CSU-Pueblo, then known as the University of Southern Colorado. This is when he met Martin Serna who was a Brown Beret with the Chicano Movement. He joined the Pueblo School Blowouts at Centennial High School which were protests to get equal treatment for Chicanos in education. He talks about Chicano Movement organizations such UMAS and MECHA that existed to also help get more equity in education. He joined La Cucaracha newspaper as a photographer. He talks about his career on the Santa Fe Railroad, first working as a trackman and later as a brakeman. He talks about how hard it was for Chicanos to be hired in positions other than laborers building track on the railroad because of prejudice and racism. He gave up the railroad and became an educator and taught at Central High School as an English & Chicano Studies instructor and a media and library specialist. He was instrumental in modernizing the classrooms with technology.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Judy Baca Oral History Interview
description: Judy Baca talks about her growing up in the neighborhood of Salt Creek in Pueblo County and the racism she experienced as a child. She discusses her time in the Naval Reserves and the leadership roles she played. Judy has been a professor at CSU-Pueblo, of social work and Chicano Studies. She talks about the obstacles she met and the successes she experienced with her programs and her students. She worked with Dr. David Sandoval, History and Chicano Studies instructor, from this expereince she gained a respect for Chicano Studies as a curriculum of its own and how important for Chicanos and non-Chicanos. She also talks about her good friend and mentor. Rita J. Martinez, a Chicana activist who made a difference in making the community aware of injustices in the legal, educational and employment opportunities for Chicanos. Judy ends her interview by sharing her vision for an expanded Chicano Studies department in the near future and what she has done to move this vision forward as the Chicano Studies Chair.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Delores Huerta stands in front of a birthday cake and lit candles, in the basement of St. Thomas Aquinas University Parish, Boulder, Colorado. Delores is wearing signature red farmworker poncho, with black “Huelga Eagle.”
description: Colorado Caucus attendees slept on a gym floor in El Paso, Texas. Caption on slide states, “LRU Natl. Convention El Paso/gym.” During conference Calderon gave an epic speech in remembrance of Ricardo Falcon.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: George Autobee Oral History Interview - Transcript
description: George Autobee talks about growing up in Pueblo and joining the marines and fighting in Viet Nam. When he returned to Pueblo he enrolled under the GI Bill at Southern Colorado State College, today CSU-Pueblo. He talks about his involvement with Chicanos for Action which became MECHA. They visited northern Colorado universities and found that they had started Chicano Studies classes. George and other MECHA members were sent to California to learn about the Chicano Studies classes there. When they returned George was MECHA president and they had to fight for Chicano Studies at SCSC by demonstrating in the administration building. Through these efforts, a Chicano Studies curriculum was started in 1972. George worked with starting Barrio Stations in the community of Pueblo which encouraged young people to graduate, stay out of trouble and get their GED’s if they needed in order to attend college. This was a successful program as was Project Adelante which also worked with the community to help youth and encourage higher education. Throughout his life, George was involved in improving the lives of Chicanos through several programs in business entrepreneurship programs for students in Pueblo and in Denver. He has helped to close the digital divide by teaching young people the importance of learning technology in order to get better paying jobs. He has become involved with the Colorado Latino Leadership Research Organization (CLLARO) previously known as LARASA founded over 50 years...

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Dr. Irene Blea Oral History Interview
description: Irene Blea talks about her childhood moving to Pueblo from New Mexico where her father got work at the steel mill. She talks about the schools in Pueblo and racism and her attending and graduating from Southern Colorado State College and going on to CU-Boulder where she got her Ph.D and became the first Chicana to teach Chicano Studies. She talks about all her success in Colorado, New Mexico and California. She also wrote at least a dozen books, nine of them textbooks for Chicano Studies, three that are fiction and one that is about the murders of young women in Albququerque without accountability. She wrote it from a parent's perspective. Irene takes pride in being a Chicana and getting the opportunities she got, even going all over the world as a presenting scholar and even went to China.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Judge Joe Ulibarri Oral History Interview -Transcript
description: Judge Joe Ulibarri discusses his life growing up in Pueblo, CO, to includes his experiences in the Bessemer and Aberdeen neighborhoods of Pueblo. He also discusses growing up in Bessemer across the street from CF&I where his dad and many members of his family members worked. He talks about the social-economic aspects in Pueblo and compare the disparities between Anglos and Mexican Americans/Hispanics/Chicanos especially. He discusses going to law school at DU through a Ford Foundation grant. He discusses the need for a Latino Chamber of Commerce and his part as a founder. He also discusses the need for a Chicano Democratic Caucus and his part as a founder. He talks about the time when he was the only Hispanic Judge in Pueblo and his part in starting a “night court" where people did not have to leave their day work to attend, especially field workers. He also talks about starting a “teen court” where students who got in trouble had trials with students as lawyers and jury members. He also talks about being the lawyer who fought School District 70 in Pueblo County when five Hispanic students were expelled because they had long hair over their years and they were not given due process. He talks about the lack of respect and racism from other judges when he first became the only Hispanic judge in Pueblo. He also talks about how Pueblo has come a long way from the discriminatory practices of the 50s, 60s, 70s, and 80s.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Dr. Ron Maestas Oral History Interview - Transcript
description: Dr. Ronald Maestas talks about growing up in the San Luis Valley and graduating from Adams State College (now Adams State University) where he earned his bachelor’s and master’s degrees and later his Ph.D. at Arizona State University in Tempe Arizona. He talks about working at Highlands University in Las Vegas New Mexico as a professor and as the Dean of Business for forty years. He applied at Adams State College and when he was interviewed, he was met by blatant discrimination and racism. He wrote, “105 Years of Discrimination,” because of incidents like this throughout his life. He remembers signs in the San Luis Valley, that read, “No dogs or Mexicans allowed.” He talks about the 1913 discrimination case against the Alamosa school where a young, boy, Miguel Antonio Maestas was told he had to go to the Mexican School instead of the American School. The Maestas family won the case and recently a statue was dedicated to the case. He talks about how Hispanics/Mexicans/Chicanos have served their country in many wars and have made the ultimate sacrifice, especially in Normandy. He also talks about how the unique Spanish language spoken in the San Luis Valley is being lost by new generations. Dr. Maestas talks about the advances Hispanics/Mexicans/Chicanos have made because of the Chicano Movement, such as the Crusade for Justice led by Corky Gonzales and the Boycott against Coors. He talks about Dolores Huerta with the farmworkers and her slogan, “Si se Puede,” meaning "yes you...

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Carmen Arteaga Oral History Interview - Transcript
description: Carmen Arteaga discusses her first-hand experience being present at the 1970 Chicano Moratorium in Los Angeles. She also talks about her mentors and how she got started writing curriculum for Chicano Studies for K-12 grades in the 1970s. She talks about her experiences in Big Max, the prison in Canon City working with the prisoners to teach them Chicano Studies and their involvement with LADS whose purpose was to discourage young Chicanos from getting into the prison system. She further talks about her experiences as a teacher within Pueblo School District 60 in the 1970s and the racism she experienced, particularly when she taught at Parkview Elementary. She discusses her involvement with student walk outs at schools like Centennial High School during the height of the Chicano Movement of the 60s and 70s. The walk outs were created to request more Chicano teachers and counselors. She goes on to discuss a class action lawsuit levied against the school district started by residents of the Salt Creek community which the Office of Civil Rights would take the case and win. The significance was that the school district was mandated to implement Bilingual and Bicultural education in elementary schools by 1975 among other requests that were mandated to offer a better education to Chicano students.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Heraldo Acosta Oral History Interview
description: Heraldo Acosta discusses what it was like growing up in Pueblo, Colorado in the 40s, 50s and 60s. He now lives in the house he was born and grew up in. The neighorhood is known as "The Blocks," near the Rawlings Library and across from Taffy's Candy Store known as the Mesa Junction. He talks about the lack of encouragement by guidance counselors for Hispanic students to go to college, so many Hispanic students went into the military. When he finished his service in the Air Force, he took advantage of the GI Bill and got a higher education including a doctorate. He moved to Berkely, California and met Huey Newton with the Black Panthers. It is here that he realized the need for a Chicano Movement. He moved back to Pueblo in the 80s and became immersed in History and Genealogy. In his research, Heraldo found that when Pueblo was incorporated in the late 1800's, General William Palmer who started the D&RGW Railroad named several streets on the Mesa Junction with Mexican names. Heraldo's research revealed in the Pueblo Chieftain that the street names were changed in 1887 to Anglo names. In the late 1990s under Heraldo's leadership the original Mexican names reappeared in brown signage underneath the green Anglo names as historcal signage references and as spatial designations.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Tom Martinez Oral History Transcript
description: Tom Martinez discusses growing up in San Francisco, Colorado and growing up on Pueblo’s East Side in the late 1950’s and 60’s. He explains the different roles he has had in the Genealogical Society of Hispanic America and the importance of documentation for genealogy and history. Mr. Martinez talks about what inspired him to become a musician and his music career while living in Kansas City, working for Hallmark Cards and how he joined the Astronomical Society of Kansas City. He shares his insights on Pueblo and his involvement with a Pueblo Heritage Museum display.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: John Valdez Oral History Interview
description: John Valdez discusses his family history in Costilla, New Mexico, moving to Avondale, Colorado, and his parents influence on his educational pursuits. He talks about his experiences as a teacher and high school and middle school counselor and how this led him to become a school administrator. Mr. Valdez shares his passion for languages, genealogy, and family traditions.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: John Nava Oral History Video
description: John Nava discusses growing up in Pueblo, Colorado on "Goat Hill" in the 1940's and 1950's. This interview covers his time in the Navy, his educational journey, career, and his experiences with discrimination. Mr. Nava was an educator for over fifty years. His past experiences helped him decide what kind of teacher he would become to best support his students. He taught several subjects including Chicano Studies and was instrumental in founding the Central High School Spanish Club and the Student Exchange Program to Hermosillo, Mexico.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: George Autobee Oral History Interview
description: George talks about registering at SCSC in the late 60s after he returned from Viet Nam, having received two Purple hearts. He discusses why he got involved in the Chicano Movement and his involvement in starting Chicano Studies at the university. He disusses going to California to study the Chicano Studies programs there and how it was emulated in Pueblo. The Adelante Program helped the cause and it set up Bario Stations in the Chicano Barrios stations in different neighborhoods to help students with their school work, sports, etc.

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Edmond Roybal Oral History Interview - Transcript
description: Edmond Roybal discusses the history of the Brown Berets in Pueblo, CO. His uniform from the Brown Berets is displayed in an exhibit next to him during this oral history interview. Edmond Roybal Oral History Interview Transcript Carmen Arteaga (CA): This is Carmen Arteaga. I am interviewing Edmond Roybal on May 16th, 2022. This interview is taking place at a Movimiento exhibit at Pueblo Community College. The interview is sponsored by Colorado State University, Pueblo and it is part of the Colorado Chicano movement. Archives. I want to confirm that Mr. Edmond Roybal understands that this interview is being recorded and that this recording will be preserved at Colorado State University Pueblo. Edmond Roybal (ER): Yes, I understand, and I agree. CA: Thank you so much for agreeing to be interviewed. The first question is, let's start by you giving us a brief biographical sketch of yourself. ER: Obviously I'm Edmond Roybal, I’ll be 70 years old, now coming in June. Born and raised on the Lower East Side. My parents were Victor and Beatrice Roybal. I was...I went to the old Centennial High School. I graduated in 1970. Never furthered my education, all I have is a high school education. I worked at medium jobs of our labor jobs of roofing and doing plumbing work. In 1974, I started working with Pueblo County Legal Services. I worked there for 41 years and retired from that program. During my time there, I was a paralegal. In 1981, I became the assistant director of Pueblo County...

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Rita Martinez Oral History Interview - Columbus Day Protests
description: Rita J. Martinez interviewed by Pablo Mora. Rita J. Martinez, Chicana activist, discusses the origins and reasons for the anti Columbus Day protests in Pueblo, Colo. Colorado State University Pueblo. University Archives and Special Collections Columbus Day Celebration and Protest Oral History Collection (ECOL) Rita J. Martinez Oral History Memoir Interviewed by Pablo Mora March 4, 2014 Colorado State University-Pueblo University Archives and Special Collections Pueblo, Colorado MORA: This is Pablo Mora, I am interviewing Rita Martinez, today, March 4th, 2014. This interview is taking place at Colorado State University-Pueblo in the library, in Pueblo, CO of course. The interview is sponsored by CSU-Pueblo University Archives and Special Collections and is part of the Southern Colorado Ethnic Heritage and Diversity Archives Project. I wanted to confirm that Ms. Martinez understands that this interview is being recorded and that this recording is being preserved at the CSU-Pueblo University Archives. MARTINEZ: I understand that. MORA: Thank you for letting me interview you. Let me start out with a little bit about you. That's what our viewers want to know. What is your background? Are you from Pueblo? MARTINEZ: I'm born and raised here in Pueblo. Yeah, I've gone to parochial schools here in Pueblo but graduated from a public school, a high school here in town. Then [I] went on to school at what was then Southern Colorado State College and then later University of Southern...

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: David Marquez Oral History Interview - Transcript
description: Dave Marquez, Chicano Studies high school and university instructor for more than thirty years talks about his growing up in Pueblo and the racism he witnessed in the schools against Chicanos. He talks about him wanting to go to college and his school counselor, in spite of very high ACT scores, was advising him to go to welding school and that it was an honorable profession. Through his perseverance and the government mandating colleges increase minority enrollment he graduated from Colorado College in Colorado Springs. Dave was directly involved with the school walkouts in Pueblo where Chicano students fought for better education, and they forced the school district to comply with several mandates. Dave talks about his respect for the Brown Berets and that it is them that are the unsung heroes of the Chicano Movement. With his Chicano Study students, they published a newspaper called La Azteca where through good journalism uncovered the miscount of the Chicano dropout rate by the school district. He talks fondly about his students and the classes he taught both at Central High School and CSU-Pueblo. Dave describes the leadership of one of his Chicano Studies students that brought about change in how the university disperses the funds they get as a Hispanic Serving Institute (HSI). Dave talks about how the Chicano Movement helped professionals advance and get elected to powerful positions in Pueblo. He ends with hope but hopes that the young people realize the struggles...

theme: "Chicano movement" OR "Chicana movement" OR "El Movimiento"
title: Cynthia Fuentes Oral History Interview - Transcript
description: Cynthia Fuentes talks about growing up in Trinidad Colorado and the start of her higher education at CSU-Fort Collins in the early 70s and moving with her husband to Pueblo, Colorado where they both graduated from CSU-Pueblo. She talks about her self-identity as a Chicana and her promotion of the Chicano Movement. She was part of several protests including the Grape Boycott. She talks about her life as an educator and with the help of Superintendent Edmund Vallejos in the Pueblo school district became one of the first elementary guidance counselors. She received her degree in elementary guidance counseling from Adams State University. The first school she was given an opportunity to work at as a counselor was Bradford Elementary under the mentorship of Lucia Martinez Rivera. She was part of a crafts program at Quinto Sol Recreation Center called "Hecho en Aztlan." She explains her definition of "Aztlan," and the richness of Mexican Folklorico dancing.

description: Description of content: Jaquez talks about his activism in the Chicano movement. , Physical description: 1 digital file in transcript form only. , Project Description: As part of the advisory committee for the exhibit El Movimiento, Daniel Salazar interviewed Carlos Santistevan on August 13, 2014. This oral history was the genesis for the 2018-2019 Movimiento Elders Project. The Movimiento Elders Project, per Salazar, was open to all interested in participating. The interviews began by asking each participant "When did you become Chicano?" with discussion following aimed to document the history of the Chicano Movement in Colorado. Organized by Salazar, himself a Colorado native and descendent of Hispanic and Mexican immigrants, the project included Salazar photographing each participant and transcribing each interview. The Movimiento Elders Project was an independent documentation project, conducted by Salazar and donated to History Colorado in 2021.

Available in audio only format on SoundCloud. , Project Description: As part of the advisory committee for the exhibit El Movimiento, Daniel Salazar interviewed Carlos Santistevan on August 13, 2014. This oral history was the genesis for the 2018-2019 Movimiento Elders Project. The Movimiento Elders Project, per Salazar, was open to all interested in participating. The interviews began by asking each participant "When did you become Chicano?" with discussion following aimed to document the history of the Chicano Movement in Colorado. Organized by Salazar, himself a Colorado native and descendent of Hispanic and Mexican immigrants, the project included Salazar photographing each participant and transcribing each interview. The Movimiento Elders Project was an independent documentation project, conducted by Salazar and donated to History Colorado in 2021.

theme: "Asian American history" OR "Asian-American history" OR "Asian American"
title: Dr. Lawrence Matsuda: On Japanese internment, Seattle in the 1950s, and the first Asian-American history class in Washington public schools
description: Lawrence Matsuda (b. 1945) is an award-winning poet, author, and educator who in 1969 started the first Asian-American history course in Washington public schools. Matsuda was born in the Japanese internment camp Minidoka after his parents were forced to the Idaho site during World War II, and grew up in Seattle, where in 1970 he worked on a Japanese American Citizens' League exhibit at the Museum of History and Industry. Matsuda contributed the name for the exhibit, "Pride and Shame," which helped inspire a national redress movement for Japanese-Americans incarcerated in wartime concentration camps.As a boy growing up on Beacon Hill, Matsuda played football with Jimi Hendrix, and as a University of Washington student he was friendly with Bruce Lee. After serving in the Army Reserves, he earned bachelor's, master's, and doctoral degrees from UW and later created the Multicultural Alumni Partnership, which has raised more than $1 million for UW scholarships. His poetry books includeA Cold Wind From IdahoandGlimpses of a Forever Foreigner. In 2017, Matsuda won a regional Emmy for a visual adaptation of his graphic novelFighting for America: Nesei Soldiers. This People's History, edited by Matsuda, is based on an interview conducted January 1, 2021 by Casey McNerthney, who was a student at Olympic Hills Elementary School in Seattle in the 1980s when Matsuda was the principal...[View full item to read remainder of encyclopedia article.]

title: Asian American publications
description: Booklist of Asian American literature. The Japanese American Relocation Collection is composed of ephemera related to the relocation program during World War II. Items include the official government report of Manzanar Relocation Center, a photo album, post-war activism materials related to preserving and remembering the camps, various clippings, and documents. The strength of this collection is found in its many perspectives on the controversial relocation program and how it has been presented since World War II.

theme: "Asian American history" OR "Asian-American history" OR "Asian American"
title: Political Transformations in the Tibetan Freedom Movement: Resettlement and Political Activism in India, Nepal, and the United States
description: This dissertation examines how Tibetan exiles were able to redefine peoplehood as displaced persons in India and Nepal and as naturalized American citizens. After the 1959 Chinese colonial occupation of Tibet, His Holiness the 14th Dalai Lama created a Tibetan Government in Exile in India to rehabilitate the Tibetan people and work towards restoring Tibet’s independence. In His Holiness’s new ‘Tibet’ operating alongside Chinese-occupied Tibet, exiles were taught to embrace and practice democracy with the national goal of regaining Tibet’s independence. Exiles believed that the Tibetan Government in Exile was the true government of Tibet and understood themselves to be refugees of Chinese invasion, despite the lack of recognition of their legal refugee status by all nation-states. However, an exiled government and a political base of followers backing an anti-colonial movement in South Asia were not enough. His Holiness looked to the United States to garner stronger support for Tibetan

theme: "Asian American history" OR "Asian-American history" OR "Asian American"
title: Asian American Bank branch, Beverly Hills
description: The highest resolution available for this image is 300 dpi.; Title supplied by cataloger. This collection is comprised of approximately 1,000 slides capturing buildings along Wilshire Boulevard. The slides were taken during Tuesday afternoon walks in 1978-1979 by Marlene Laskey and her 15-year-old daughter, Annie, who was the primary photographer. Marlene (1933-1989) was a Michigan native who moved to Los Angeles in the 1950s and became a Los Angeles historian and architecture enthusiast. At the time of her death from cancer, Marlene was working on a Master of Urban Planning degree at UCLA, and was an interviewer for UCLA's Oral History program. Looking across the street towards a branch for Asian American Bank (no longer standing), located at 9090 Wilshire Boulevard in Beverly Hills. A large anchor sculpture is seen on the left and the office building, opened in 1968, at 9100 Wilshire Boulevard is seen on the right.

theme: "Asian American history" OR "Asian-American history" OR "Asian American"
title: Asian American strawberry picker, ca. 1919
description: Many Asian immigrants in the Seattle and King County area were farmers. Before World War I, Japanese farmers were already supplying most of the region's population with vegetables, small fruits, and diary products. Starting in the 1920s, many immigrants from the Philippines worked as seasonal laborers at area farms. In this photo, taken in the Seattle area around 1919, an Asian-American strawberry picker shows off her flat of strawberries. Rows of strawberry plants surround her in the field. 1 negative: nitrate, b&w; 8 x 10 in.

title: Asian American poetry reading, Richmond Library
description: The black and white poster features a drawing of three different groups of people in various states of action. The first group includes a young girl standing with her hand over her heart, a man in military uniform, a portrait of a woman wearing a kimono, and barbed wire. The next grouping includes three men in various poses. The last grouping depicts three men, each wearing hats. The text states, "Asian American Poetry Reading, Richmond Library, 351 9th Avenue, Tuesday, January 25, 7:00 pm, Ed Bedajos, Norman Jayo, Janice Mirikitani, Lou Syquia, Prescal Tabios, Lane Nishikawa, Ginny Lim, Rovena Ignacio, George Leong, Joselyn Ignacio, Terri Lee, Al Roble, Doug Yamamoto."

theme: "Transgender" OR "Transsexual" OR "Gender identity" OR "Gender nonconforming" OR "Genderqueer" OR "Transgender people"
title: Development and Validation of the Objectified Body Consciousness Scales for Transgender, Nonbinary, and Gender-Nonconforming Individuals (OBCT)
description: Introduction: Objectification theory originally asserted that cisgender women and girls are socialized to internalize an observer’s perspective of their appearance, which yields a pattern of self-objectification (Fredrickson & Roberts, 1997; McKinley & Hyde, 1996). Objectification theory offers a compelling framework for understanding body experiences among TNG individuals, particularly with regards to the broader dehumanization process (Moradi, 2010; Moradi, 2013). One aspect of this theory is objectified body consciousness, which describes the internalization of societal body standards and the belief that they are self-originating, achievable, and duty-bound (i.e., that one is responsible for achieving them). The original Objectified Body Consciousness scale (OBC; McKinley & Hyde, 1996) is a powerful measure for cisgender women and girls, but has not demonstrated validity among transgender, nonbinary, and gender nonconforming individuals (TNG) (Rosenkranz & Barr, 2016). Specific Aims

theme: "Transgender" OR "Transsexual" OR "Gender identity" OR "Gender nonconforming" OR "Genderqueer" OR "Transgender people"
title: The Logic of Gender
description: In this presentation, Professor Kelli Potter considers two seemingly unrelated topics: logic and gender. The dominant view in the West is that logic is unassailable and impartial. This view scoffs at the idea that logic could be political or biased in any way. However, this view is mistaken. Logic is political. And Western or “classical” logic is one that justifies the domination of marginalized groups. Classical logic is binary, involving two mutually exclusive and exhaustive categories: the True and the False. It sees the world as black and white, always privileging one category over another. This strict binary is imbedded in our conceptual frameworks, including that of gender. And the gender binary justifies not only the oppression of women, but also of transgender and intersex people. How should we deal with this problem? Do we have to abandon logic? Or is it possible to construct a logic that isn’t oppressive, but rather liberating? Professor Potter attempts to answer t
